student_name: Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari
hub_subtitle: Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

lesson.menu_title: Navigating the Unexpected -- When You Don't Understand or Need Time
lesson.menu_desc: Stay calm and in control when a conversation gets confusing: everyday words for clarifying like to clarify, to rephrase, to catch, vague, filler and to recap, plus the repair strategies that ask for repetition, buy you time and check you understood -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 18 -- Navigating the Unexpected
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari | Lesson 18 -- Navigating the Unexpected
lesson.grammar_point: clarification and repair strategies (asking for repetition, buying time)

vocab.title: Match each clarifying word to its meaning
defs: to make something clearer or easier to understand
to say the same thing again using different words
to hear and understand what someone says
to be clear and easy to understand
to pause before speaking because you are not sure
a small word like well or um that gives you time to think
to repeat the main points quickly
to ask someone to be patient and wait
to wait for a short moment
simple, clear and easy to understand

paras: Last week I called a health clinic to book a checkup. The receptionist was friendly, but she spoke very fast. She gave me an address, a floor number and a time, all in one breath, and I did not catch half of it. My old instinct was to say yes, thank her and hang up, even though her instructions were confusing and a little vague. But I did not want to arrive on the wrong day, so this time I decided to ask.

"Sorry, could you clarify the time?" I said. She kindly repeated it. When one word still did not make sense, I asked her to rephrase it in simpler English. I was nervous, so I used a filler -- "Well, let me see..." -- to hold on for a second and think. "Bear with me," I added, "my English is still improving." She waited, calm and patient, and slowly everything became clear.

Before we finished, I did a quick recap: I repeated the address and the time back to her, and she said they were correct. I used to hesitate and just pretend, but I have learned that asking is not rude -- it is smart. When something is confusing, a short, straightforward question turns a stressful moment into a simple one. So when you do not understand, do not nod and hope. Clarify, rephrase, buy a little time, and check. That is how a real conversation stays on track.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 reading, a learner uses clarification and repair strategies to stay in control of a confusing phone call

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: It is a list of the exact prices and opening hours of a health clinic.
When Ana Paula does not understand, she now asks people to clarify, repeat or rephrase, instead of pretending -- and it keeps the conversation on track.
It argues that you should never book an appointment by phone in English.

The text says: 'she spoke very fast' and gave everything 'all in one breath'.

The text says: 'My old instinct was to say yes, thank her and hang up.'

The text says: 'I asked her to rephrase it in simpler English.'

gapfill.text: When a conversation gets hard, you have simple tools called repair strategies. To ask for repetition, say "Sorry, could you say that again?" To ask for meaning, say "What do you mean by that?" To buy time, use a filler (well, let me see) or say "Let me think for a second." To check you understood, recap: repeat the main points back. Asking someone to clarify or rephrase is not a weakness -- it is exactly what confident speakers do.

bank.label: Phrases that keep you in control of a conversation
items: Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you say it again?
What do you mean by that, exactly?
Let me think for a second.
Hold on -- do you mean today or tomorrow?
So, just to recap, the appointment is at three.
Bear with me, my English is still improving.
Sorry, that part was a little confusing for me.

lf.title: What is each phrase doing?
items: a second -- buy time to think.

lf.text: Notice the four jobs: ask to repeat (say that again), ask for meaning (what do you mean by...?), buy time (a filler like well, or let me think), and check understanding (recap the main points). Keep the tone polite and calm -- start with Sorry or Could you, and the request sounds friendly, not rude.

items.situation: A friend gives you their new address very fast on the phone. Ask them to repeat it.
tips: Sorry, could you say that again?
I didn't catch the address -- could you repeat it?

items.situation: Someone uses a word you do not know. Ask what it means.
tips: What do you mean by that?
Sorry, what does that word mean?

items.situation: A colleague asks you a hard question and you need a moment. Buy some time.
tips: That is a good question -- let me think for a second.
Well, hold on... let me see.

items.situation: The instructions were long. Check that you understood by repeating the main points.
tips: So, just to recap, we meet at three, right?
Let me recap to make sure I understood.

items.situation: A person is speaking too fast and it is confusing. Politely ask them to rephrase.
tips: Could you rephrase that more simply?
Sorry, that was a little confusing -- could you say it another way?

items: I will say three sentences too fast, or with one hard word. For each one, ask me to repeat it or to explain the word.
I will ask you three simple questions. Before you answer, buy a second with a filler like well or let me think.
I will give you a short set of plans -- a place, a day and a time. Listen, then recap them back to me to check you understood.
We have a normal chat, but I will suddenly speak fast and use one confusing word. Stay calm, ask me to clarify or rephrase, and keep the conversation going.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model phrases
list: Ask to repeat: Sorry, could you say that again? / I didn't catch that.
Ask for meaning: What do you mean by that? / Sorry, what does that word mean?
Buy time: Let me think for a second. / Well... let me see.
Ask for simpler words: Could you rephrase that in simpler English?
Check / recap: So, just to recap...? / So you mean..., right?
answerkey.note: These repair strategies keep any conversation on track. Asking is not a weakness -- it is what confident speakers do.

listenings.text: Here is something I wish someone had told me years ago: it is completely normal not to understand every word, even in your own language. When a person speaks too fast and you do not catch something, you do not have to pretend. Just ask. You can say, Sorry, could you say that again? Or, if a word is confusing, ask them to rephrase it in simpler English. If you need a moment to think, use a filler like well or let me see, and hold on for a second. And before you finish, do a quick recap: repeat the main points back to check. Asking people to clarify is not a weakness. It is the most confident thing a good communicator does.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I have to tell you what happened on the phone this morning. I called the dentist, and the receptionist gave me the new address so fast that I did not catch the street number at all. The old me would have just said okay and hoped for the best. But this time I said, Sorry, could you clarify that? and she happily repeated it. One part was still vague, so I asked her to rephrase it, and then, just to recap, I read the whole address back to her. It took thirty extra seconds, and now I actually know where to go! Honestly, do not hesitate to ask people to slow down. It works every time. Okay, call me back when you can. Bye!

extra_audio.text: Last week I called a clinic to book a checkup. The receptionist spoke fast, and I did not catch the time. I asked her to clarify and to rephrase it in simpler words. I used a filler to hold on and think for a second. To recap, I repeated the address back to check I understood.
